attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of darkened patches on her face. She is 24 weeks pregnant with her first child. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins. She works as a primary school teacher and spends time outdoors during playground supervision.

On examination, she appears well though tired, with vital signs within normal limits. There are symmetrical brown-grey patches on her cheeks, forehead, and upper lip. The patches have irregular but well-defined borders. The remainder of her skin examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of melasma, also known as chloasma gravidarum or the 'mask of pregnancy'. The condition is characterised by symmetrical brown-grey patches or spotswith irregular but well-defined borders, typically affecting sun-exposed areas of the face. The onset during pregnancy is typical, as hormonal changes combined with sun exposure are key triggers.

Melasma can affect women during pregnancy, those with Fitzpatrick phototypes 3 - 4, multiparity, thyroid disorders, family history of melasma in pregnancy, or those taking oral contraceptives or anticonvulsants. The characteristic distribution includes the cheeks, forehead, and upper lip in a symmetrical pattern. The condition is exacerbated by sun exposure and usually improves post-pregnancy though can persist for years after delivery.

Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation occurs following inflammation or injury to the skin and typically has a more irregular distribution corresponding to the site of previous inflammation. There is no history of preceding skin inflammation in this case.

Actinic lentigo presents as well-circumscribed brown macules that are usually smaller and more discrete than melasma. They are more common in older individuals with chronic sun damage and do not present with the characteristic symmetrical distribution seen in melasma.

Addison disease can cause hyperpigmentation but typically affects the whole body, including non-sun-exposed areas, mucous membranes, and palmar creases. Patients usually have systemic symptoms such as fatigue, weight loss, and postural hypotension. Fatigue is a non-specific symptoms and likely is secondary to the pregnancy in this case.

Tinea versicolor, caused by Malassezia species, typically presents with scaly patches that can be hyperpigmented or hypopigmented. It more commonly affects the trunk and proximal extremities rather than the face, and has a fine scale that can be demonstrated by scratching the surface.
learningPoints: Melasma presents as symmetrical brown-grey patches on sun-exposed areas, particularly common during pregnancy.
Diagnosis of melasma is clinical, based on characteristic appearance and distribution of hyperpigmentation.
references: Fathabad M, Raesi R, Hushmandi K, Hosseini M, Soleimani A, Daneshi S. Clinical and Epidemiological Features of Melasma in Women of Iran: A Cross-sectional Study. Open Public Health Journal, 2024.  Available at: https://openpublichealthjournal.com/VOLUME/17/ELOCATOR/e18749445311116/FULLTEXT/

Zhao L, Fu X, Cheng H. Prevention of Melasma During Pregnancy: Risk Factors and Photoprotection-Focused Strategies. Clin Cosmet Investig Dermatol. 2024 Oct 15;17:2301-2310.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11490249/
